finding: Learners acquire associations only between elements that receive joint attentional processing, irrespective of whether those elements are spatially or temporally contiguous. Objective contiguity alone is insufficient for learning when elements are not jointly attended.

Dissociations between priming and recognition performance in amnesia and normal adults can arise from a single memory source subjected to independent task-specific noise, without requiring separate memorial systems.

Bilinguals and monolinguals do not differ in active inhibitory efficiency but differ in how strongly they maintain and use goal representations, leading to task-dependent reactive inhibition of irrelevant information.

Semantic relations between prime words automatically modulate obligatory activation of arithmetic facts in proportion to the alignment between semantic structure and operation structure, not merely by strength of relatedness.

Activation spreads continuously from concepts to phonological forms in word production, but the amount spreading is limited and task-dependent. Picture naming engages conceptual access and full cascade; word reading bypasses concepts and limits cascade.

Visual working memory contents remain volatile and susceptible to interference unless focused attention is directed to a specific item, consolidating that item into a more durable representation resistant to probe interference.
